landmarks.landmark_blurb: Her father built this house, later moved to Historic Deerfield.

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, beside her husband Judge John Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Born to Springfield's mercantile gentry, 1764
narrative_blocks.body: She was born 13 December 1764 to Col. Josiah Dwight — Yale 1736, owner of an iron foundry, a potash manufactory, and 5,000 acres of Hampshire County land — and to Elizabeth Buckminster of Brookfield. Her father died in 1768 leaving an estate worth £9,458 when she was four; her mother survived to 1798.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Judge John Hooker, December 1790
narrative_blocks.body: On 18 December 1790 in Springfield she married John Hooker, Yale 1782 graduate and son of the Rev. John of Northampton. She bore him ten children between 1791 and 1808, including Dr. Worthington Hooker (H01407) and Elizabeth Dwight Hooker Packard (H01403).

grandchildren.n: George Hooker (infant son of Dr. George)
Unnamed son of Worthington & Henrietta Hooker

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Her father built this house, later moved to Historic Deerfield.

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, beside her husband Judge John Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Born to Springfield's mercantile gentry, 1764
narrative_blocks.body: She was born 13 December 1764 to Col. Josiah Dwight — Yale 1736, owner of an iron foundry, a potash manufactory, and 5,000 acres of Hampshire County land — and to Elizabeth Buckminster of Brookfield. Her father died in 1768 leaving an estate worth £9,458 when she was four; her mother survived to 1798.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Judge John Hooker, December 1790
narrative_blocks.body: On 18 December 1790 in Springfield she married John Hooker, Yale 1782 graduate and son of the Rev. John of Northampton. She bore him ten children between 1791 and 1808, including Dr. Worthington Hooker (H01407) and Elizabeth Dwight Hooker Packard (H01403).

cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law; Richest man in old Hampshire County

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1782, Springfield lawyer and judge
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated from Yale College in 1782 and settled at Springfield, MA, where he practiced law and rose to Judge of the Court of Common Pleas and then Judge of Probate at his death. He was a Deacon of the First Congregational Church of Springfield throughout this period.

narrative_blocks.header: Member of American Board for Foreign Missions
narrative_blocks.body: He was an active member of the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM), the first US foreign-missions organization, founded 1810 in Williamstown MA. The Board sent New England missionaries to India, the Sandwich Islands (Hawaii), and Native American tribes — a defining institution of New England Federalist evangelical culture.

education.notes: Son of John Hooker (H00527, Yale 1782). Lawyer, Springfield MA.

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, Hampden County MA — with wife Rachel Breck and his parents Judge John (H00527) and Sarah Dwight Hooker

education.notes: Studied medicine at the Medical Institution of Yale College, receiving M.D. in 1817. Practiced afterward with Dr. Eli Ives in New Haven.

career.notes: Practiced with Dr. Eli Ives — Yale's Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine, the same chair later occupied (1852-1867) by George's younger brother Worthington Hooker (H01407).
Removed to Syracuse, NY where he practiced medicine for several years.
Returned east to Longmeadow MA where he continued medical practice until his death there 14 March 1884 at age 91.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1817 M.D. under Dr. Eli Ives
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale College 1814 he took his medical degree from the Medical Institution of Yale College in 1817, then practiced alongside Dr. Eli Ives in New Haven. Ives — Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine at Yale until 1852 — would later be succeeded in that chair by George's younger brother Worthington Hooker (H01407).

narrative_blocks.header: Syracuse practice, then Longmeadow homecoming
narrative_blocks.body: After his New Haven training he removed to Syracuse, New York, where he practiced medicine for some years before returning east to Longmeadow, Massachusetts. He continued practice at Longmeadow until his death there 14 March 1884 at age 91.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived ninety-one years to 1884
narrative_blocks.body: He was born 7 March 1793 to Judge John Hooker (H00527) and Sarah Dwight Hooker (X00227) at Springfield, and lived past his ninety-first birthday — dying just seven days after it on 14 March 1884 at Longmeadow. He had outlived his celebrated younger brother Dr. Worthington Hooker (H01407) by seventeen years.

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, Hampden County MA, with husband Dr. George Hooker

parents.father_name: Joseph Hunt Breck (1766-1801) of Northampton MA — died when Rachel was 9
parents.mother_name: Abigail Kingsley Breck (c.1766-1846) of Northampton MA

education.notes: Attended three twelve-week sessions beginning 11 April 1806 at Deerfield Academy, approximately 17 miles north of Northampton.

Attended Misses Patten's School in Hartford CT at age 18. Curriculum: Latin, Greek, English, French, mercantile arithmetic, bookkeeping, geography, astronomy, natural philosophy, writing, composition, drawing, painting, embroidery, filigree, and ornamental work. Stitched the 'Charity' silk embroidered picture (ART055) there in 1810; companion sampler from earlier years also extant in Smithsonian collection.

artworks.blurb: Silk embroidered 'Charity' (1810) now in Smithsonian National Museum of American History; donated 1951 by Greenwood collectors.

narrative_blocks.header: Bore eight children, four survived adulthood
narrative_blocks.body: From the Breck family of Northampton MA, she married Dr. George Hooker around 1819 and bore eight children between 1820 and 1833. Of those eight only four survived to adulthood — three daughters died young (two infants and one age 5), one son died at 29 — while she herself lived to age 86, dying at Longmeadow in 1879.

narrative_blocks.header: Father dead at thirty-five, she was nine
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Joseph Hunt Breck of Northampton died in 1801 at age thirty-five when she was nine, leaving her mother Abigail Kingsley Breck a widow at thirty-five with a young daughter. Abigail did not remarry and lived another forty-five years, dying in 1846 at age eighty.

narrative_blocks.header: Stitched at eighteen, now in the Smithsonian
narrative_blocks.body: In 1810 at age eighteen, attending the Misses Patten's School in Hartford CT, she stitched a silk embroidered picture of Charity from a mezzotint engraving. Donated by collectors in 1951, it now lives in the Textile Collection of the National Museum of American History — reunited with her earlier Northampton sampler in the museum's first paired example of a girl's two needlework levels.

narrative_blocks.header: Sarah died young
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah died in 1825, aged about five. Her parents gave the name to a sister born three years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Lucy died young
narrative_blocks.body: Lucy died in 1823, aged about one.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived ninety-six years, the family's longest-lived
narrative_blocks.body: She was born 1824 at Longmeadow to Dr. George Hooker and Rachel Breck Hooker, and survived until 1920 — past her ninety-sixth birthday. She was the longest-lived of all George and Rachel's eight children, outliving even her father's 91 years by five.

narrative_blocks.header: Long Meadow probate judge, married 1855
narrative_blocks.body: He was born 5 June 1826 at Long Meadow and married Ellen Eliza Bliss of Long Meadow on 2 October 1855 in Connecticut. Three children — Harriet Breck (H04329), Mary Dwight (H04330), and George Bliss (H04331) — were born at Elmira NY in the late 1850s and early 1860s before the family returned to Long Meadow.

bio.display_name: George Hooker (infant son of Dr. George)

narrative_blocks.header: George died young
narrative_blocks.body: George died in 1831, aged about one.

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, Hampden County MA — with parents and grandparents

cross_connections.display_label: First cousin Almira was Dr. Enoch Hale's first wife

narrative_blocks.header: Married her first cousin Almira's widower, 1822
narrative_blocks.body: In May 1822, Sarah Hooker married Dr. Enoch Hale — the Westhampton physician who had been widowed six years earlier by her first cousin Almira Hooker. The two women shared the same Thomas Hooker lineage: Sarah through her father Judge John Hooker of Springfield, Almira through her father Dr. William Hooker of Westhampton. Sarah died at Westhampton three years later on April 25, 1825.

parents.father_research_notes: Rev. Enoch Hale (X02859), first minister of West Hampton MA, elder brother of Nathan Hale per EH 1909 #1410.
parents.mother_research_notes: Octavia Throop Hale — daughter of Rev. Enoch and Octavia Throop Hale of Bozrah CT per EH 1909 #1410.

career.notes: Per EH 1909 #1412: 'Enoch Hale, M.D.' Son of Rev. Enoch Hale, first minister of West Hampton.

cross_connections.display_label: Uncle; Continental Army spy executed by British, 1776
His nephew Henry Hooker married a Hale first cousin, 1844

narrative_blocks.header: Married two Hooker first cousins in sequence
narrative_blocks.body: After his first wife Almira Hooker died at Gardiner Maine in December 1816, Dr. Enoch Hale married Sarah Hooker in 1822 — Almira's own first cousin, daughter of Judge John Hooker of Springfield. Sarah died three years later in 1825; Enoch's third wife Jane Murdock, whom he married in 1827, survived him by forty-three years.

narrative_blocks.header: Nathan Hale's execution shadowed his Westhampton childhood
narrative_blocks.body: His father Rev. Enoch Hale was the elder brother of Capt. Nathan Hale, the Continental Army officer executed as a spy by the British in September 1776. Dr. Enoch Hale grew up in a household that lived under the shadow of his famous uncle's martyrdom.

burial.plot_notes: Returned to Springfield Cemetery, beside her parents Judge John and Sarah Dwight Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Married Frederic Packard in Springfield, 1822
narrative_blocks.body: On 15 May 1822 she married Frederic Adolphus Packard, a Harvard 1814 graduate then editing the Hampden Federalist in Springfield. The couple moved to Philadelphia in 1829 when he became editor of American Sunday School Union publications, ultimately producing 2,000 titles including the first child's newspaper in the United States.

narrative_blocks.header: Bore five Packard children; two died young
narrative_blocks.body: Five children followed between 1827 and 1836: John Hooker Packard 1st (H02742, died on his second birthday); Frederick Jr. (H02743, lawyer Appleton WI); Mary Hooker (H02744, married Samuel Clarke Perkins of Skull and Bones); Dr. John Hooker Packard (H02745, the eminent Philadelphia surgeon); and Lewis Richard (H02746, Yale Hillhouse Professor of Greek). The eldest two were buried back in Springfield with the Hooker grandparents.

narrative_blocks.header: Died Philadelphia 1862, returned to Springfield
narrative_blocks.body: She died 5 July 1862 in Philadelphia at age 64, where she had lived since 1829. Her body was returned to Springfield Cemetery to lie beside her father Judge John and her Hooker grandparents — five years before her husband Frederic would join them in 1867.

parents.father_name: Rev. Asa Packard, pastor of Congregational church in Marlborough MA; descendant of Samuel Packard, founder of Bridgewater

notable.notable_blurb: American Sunday School Union editor of 2,000 publications

institutions.institution_blurb: Harvard College, A.B. 1814. Studied law at Northampton after graduation.

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, with wife Elizabeth Dwight Hooker Packard

education.notes: Harvard 1814. Studied law at Northampton after graduation; practiced at Springfield 1817-1829.

career.notes: Practiced law at Springfield, Massachusetts.
Edited the Federalist newspaper for approximately 10 years.

Edited all 2,000 publications of the ASS Union; wrote or compiled 40+ himself. Edited the Sunday-School Magazine, Sunday-School Journal, and Youth's Penny Gazette (renamed the Child's World). Edited 11 volumes of the Philadelphia Journal of Prison Discipline.

narrative_blocks.header: Edited Hampden Federalist in Springfield, 1817-1827
narrative_blocks.body: After Harvard 1814 he studied law at Northampton and practiced at Springfield, MA from 1817 to 1829. For ten of those years he edited the Hampden Federalist newspaper alongside his law practice.

narrative_blocks.header: Edited 2,000 Sunday School Union publications
narrative_blocks.body: From 1829 until his death in 1867 he edited the publications of the American Sunday School Union — 2,000 titles in all, more than forty of which he wrote or compiled himself. He prepared the Society's annual reports and contributed regularly to its Magazine, Journal, and Penny Gazette.

narrative_blocks.header: Projected the first child's newspaper in America
narrative_blocks.body: He projected the Youth's Penny Gazette (later renamed the Child's World), by his own description the first newspaper in the United States designed specifically for children. The paper ran weekly through the American Sunday School Union network.

narrative_blocks.header: Declined Girard College presidency, July 1849
narrative_blocks.body: In July 1849 he was elected president of Girard College in Philadelphia — a then-new and well-endowed institution founded for poor white orphan boys — and declined. He preferred to continue his publishing work and education advocacy from his Philadelphia perch.

narrative_blocks.header: Mass. legislature 1828-29 between Springfield careers
narrative_blocks.body: He served as a member of the Massachusetts legislature in 1828-29, his last public role in the state before removing to Philadelphia. The legislative session preceded his elevation to American Sunday School Union editorship by a single year.

narrative_blocks.header: John died young
narrative_blocks.body: John died in 1829 at the age of two. His parents gave the name to a brother born three years later.

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, with parents and Hooker grandparents

career.notes: Prominent citizen of Appleton, Wisconsin. Practiced law there until his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Lawyer at Appleton, Wisconsin frontier
narrative_blocks.body: After marrying Ellen Louisa Hall in Medford, MA on 2 November 1852, the couple moved to Appleton, Wisconsin where he established a law practice and became a prominent citizen. He died at Philadelphia 18 July 1862 — the same year his mother Elizabeth died — leaving behind an adopted daughter, Fanny Weed.

narrative_blocks.header: Adopted Fanny Weed; no biological children
narrative_blocks.body: He and Ellen Louisa had no children of their own and adopted a girl who became known as Fanny Weed Packard. She later married Rev. Charles Richardson, carrying the Packard name forward in name only.

burial.plot_notes: Laurel Hill East Cemetery, Philadelphia, with husband Samuel Clarke Perkins

narrative_blocks.header: Married Samuel Clarke Perkins in Philadelphia, 1854
narrative_blocks.body: On 12 April 1854 she married Samuel Clarke Perkins, a Philadelphia lawyer and Yale class of 1848. Perkins was a member of Yale's Skull and Bones society and later served as a Freemason Grand Master in Philadelphia.

notable.primary_url_label: Dr. Echols & Dr. Arbittier — John H. Packard, M.D.

bio.title: Doctor
bio.photo_notes: Portrait photograph reproduced in Anesthesiology journal, Nov 2001 (Defalque, Panning, Wright).

burial.plot_notes: Old Saint David's Church Cemetery, Wayne, Delaware County, PA

education.notes: A.B. 1850, M.D. 1853. Resident physician at Philadelphia's University Hospital for 18 months after graduation; then 1.5 years visiting the great Paris hospitals before returning to Philadelphia practice.

career.notes: Acting assistant surgeon at Christian Street and Satterlee Union Army hospitals during the Civil War.

career.organization: U.S. Army hospitals at Beverly NJ and Haddington PA

career.notes: Surgeon to Pennsylvania Hospital from 1884 until his 1896 retirement following septicemia from a finger infection caught during surgery.
Secretary 1862-1877; Vice President from 1886.

narrative_blocks.header: Switched American surgeons from chloroform to ether
narrative_blocks.body: In May 1865 he reported to the Philadelphia College of Physicians that after witnessing fatal chloroform accidents in his own and his colleagues' practices, he was switching to ether. Following his friend F. D.

narrative_blocks.header: Manual of Minor Surgery adopted by US Army
narrative_blocks.body: His 1863 Manual of Minor Surgery — a working guide to bandaging, pain control, and field operations — was adopted by the United States Army and became one of the most reprinted surgical handbooks of the Civil War era. The Handbook of Operative Surgery followed in 1870.

narrative_blocks.header: Civil War surgeon at Christian Street and Satterlee
narrative_blocks.body: He served as acting assistant surgeon at Philadelphia's Christian Street and Satterlee U.S. Army hospitals throughout the Civil War. One soldier whose arm Packard rebuilt — Martin V.B. Keller — wrote afterward that the surgeon was 'very proud of the operation' and had photographs taken of the recovered arm.

narrative_blocks.header: Septic finger ended career, 1896 retirement
narrative_blocks.body: A finger infection caught during surgery in 1896 led to septicemia and forced his retirement at sixty-four after forty-three years of practice. He died of cardiorenal failure at the Hotel Chalfont in Atlantic City on 21 May 1907 and was buried at Old Saint David's Church Cemetery in Wayne, PA.

narrative_blocks.header: Sea-Air therapy book established resort medicine
narrative_blocks.body: His 1880 book Sea-Air and Sea-Bathing was among the first American medical works to argue systematically that coastal air and bathing could benefit specific patient categories. The treatise helped establish the Atlantic City and Cape May resort culture that would shape East Coast medicine for a generation.

notable.notable_blurb: Hillhouse Professor of Greek at Yale

education.notes: Yale 1856 then extended study at University of Berlin where he received Ph.D. First studied theology after Berlin but gave it up on account of ill health.

career.notes: Began as tutor, then Assistant Professor (1863), then Professor of the Greek language and literature (1866); ultimately appointed Hillhouse Professor.
First director sent to take charge of the new Athens-based archaeological school established by American colleges.

narrative_blocks.header: Hillhouse Professor of Greek at Yale
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale 1856 and a Berlin Ph.D., he became first tutor, then Assistant Professor (1863), then Professor (1866) of Greek at Yale, ultimately appointed Hillhouse Professor. He had originally studied for the ministry but gave it up on account of ill health.

narrative_blocks.header: First director, American school in Athens, 1883
narrative_blocks.body: In 1883 he was sent to Athens to take charge of the archaeological school recently established there by the American colleges. He died at New Haven, Connecticut, the following year on 26 October 1884; seven of his essays and lectures were collected and published as a memorial volume.

cross_connections.display_label: Prof. Worthington Hooker married Henrietta Whitney Edwards, great-granddaughter of Jonathan.
DOUBLE HOOKER DESCENT: His second wife Henrietta Whitney Edwards was.
DOUBLE HOOKER DESCENT: His second wife Henrietta Whitney Edwards was.
Both buried Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven — Hooker descendants
was a later Yale physician who pioneered cardiac defibrillation

notable.notable_blurb: physician, Yale Professor of Theory and Practice of

narrative_blocks.header: Physician and Patient — landmark US medical ethics
narrative_blocks.body: His 1849 book Physician and Patient has been described as the most original United States contribution to medical ethics in the nineteenth century. It argued for honesty with patients, condemned medical deception even when well-intentioned, and shaped American physician-patient communication norms for a generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine
narrative_blocks.body: After twenty-three years practicing medicine in Norwich, Connecticut, he was chosen in 1852 to succeed Dr. Eli Ives as Yale's Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine. He held the chair until his death, combining teaching with active medical practice and continued publication.

narrative_blocks.header: Vice President of American Medical Association, 1864
narrative_blocks.body: He was elected Vice President of the American Medical Association in 1864 — among the highest national positions in mid-19th-century American medicine. The AMA at that time was the leading professional voice for orthodox medicine against homeopathy, eclectic medicine, and the prison sanitary movement.

narrative_blocks.header: Worthington Hooker School named for him
narrative_blocks.body: The Worthington Hooker School in New Haven's East Rock neighborhood still bears his name and is part of the city's public school system. The school was named in recognition of his Yale professorship and his presence in New Haven's intellectual establishment.

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin marriage united two Hooker branches
narrative_blocks.body: His second marriage on 31 January 1855 to Henrietta Whitney Edwards — daughter of Connecticut Governor Henry W. Edwards — also reunited two branches of the Hooker family. Henrietta descended from Mary Hooker (1673-1740) through her great-great-grandmother Sarah Pierpont and her great-great-grandfather Rev. James Pierpont, making her and Worthington both seventh-generation descendants of Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Shaped by Silliman at Yale, Müller in Berlin
narrative_blocks.body: His Yale years (graduated 1825) put him under Benjamin Silliman, the chemistry and natural history professor who championed empirical observation as the basis of American science. Later study of the German anatomist Johannes Müller's comparative physiology shaped Hooker's textbooks on Human Physiology and his rationalist defense of evidence-based therapeutics against homeopathy.

narrative_blocks.header: Brothers framed Eli Ives at Yale, 1817-1852
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother George took the same Yale M.D. degree in 1817 and apprenticed directly with Dr. Eli Ives, then Yale's Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine. Thirty-five years later in 1852, Worthington was chosen to succeed Ives in that same Yale chair when Ives retired — making the Hooker brothers the bookends of one of New Haven's longest medical legacies.

education.notes: Medical studies in Philadelphia and Cambridge; Harvard M.D. 1829.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Public elementary and middle school in New Haven CT named after him; original 180 Canner Street building erected 1900; new middle school campus opened 2009.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven, CT — gravestone reads 'Beloved Physician'

career.notes: Practiced medicine in Norwich, Connecticut for 23 years before his Yale appointment. His wife Mary Ingersoll died here in early 1853 just months after the family relocated to New Haven.

career.role: Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine
career.notes: Succeeded Dr. Eli Ives in the chair when Ives resigned. Held the professorship until his death; combined teaching with active medical practice and continued publication.
Elected VP of the AMA in 1864 — among the highest national positions in mid-19th-century American medicine.

sources.short: The Ingersolls of Hampshire (Ingersoll family genealogy), entry #98

parents.father_name: John Ingersoll II (1769-1840) of Westfield, Springfield, Savannah GA, and New York City — merchant
parents.mother_name: Elisabeth Martin (1779-1868), survived her daughter Mary by 15 years
parents.mother_research_notes: Per FamilySearch tree (FamilySearch ID KG3H-PHK). NOT the same Elizabeth Martin as X00228 in our tree (X00228 = wife of H00494 Samuel Hooker b.1762, married 1790; Elisabeth Martin Ingersoll b.1779 would have been 11 at that date, ruling out identity).

narrative_blocks.header: Westfield Ingersoll merchant family, married 1830
narrative_blocks.body: She was born 26 April 1808 at Westfield, Massachusetts to John Ingersoll, and married Worthington Hooker on 29 September 1830 in Springfield. Her brother Job Ingersoll later named his youngest son Worthington Hooker Ingersoll (b.31 Oct 1862) in honor of his accomplished brother-in-law.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: School in New Haven named for her husband Worthington Hooker; she did not live to see it (built 1900, she died 1853).

cross_connections.notes: Previously unrecognized: X00160 was classified as tier 2 (non-descendant spouse) but is a confirmed Hooker descendant gen 6, reclassified in v0.3.32.
cross_connections.display_label: DOUBLE HOOKER DESCENT MARRIAGE: Henrietta Whitney Edwards and Prof. Worthington.

cross_connections.notes: Previously unrecognized: X00160 was classified as tier 2 (non-descendant spouse) but is a confirmed Hooker descendant gen 6, reclassified in v0.3.32.
cross_connections.display_label: DOUBLE HOOKER DESCENT MARRIAGE: Henrietta Whitney Edwards and Prof. Worthington.

cross_connections.notes: Previously unrecognized: X00160 was classified as tier 2 (non-descendant spouse) but is a confirmed Hooker descendant gen 6, reclassified in v0.3.32.
cross_connections.display_label: DOUBLE HOOKER DESCENT MARRIAGE: Henrietta Whitney Edwards and Prof. Worthington.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: School in New Haven named for her husband Worthington Hooker; built 1900, six years after her own 1894 death.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT, with husband Worthington

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin marriage united two Hooker branches
narrative_blocks.body: She and Worthington Hooker (H01407) were both seventh-generation descendants of Thomas Hooker when they married 31 January 1855 — Worthington via the direct John Hooker paternal line, Henrietta via Sarah Pierpont (HD0418) and Rev. James Pierpont's marriage to Mary Hooker (H00034) in the 1690s. The couple had no children together, leaving the dual descent uncombined in any biological line.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Connecticut Governor Henry W. Edwards
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Gov. Henry Waggaman Edwards (HD2045) served Connecticut as governor 1833-34 and 1835-38, after representing the state in both the US Senate (1823-27) and House of Representatives (1819-23). His mother Frances Ogden was a cousin of Aaron Burr, making Henrietta a cousin once removed of the third Vice President of the United States.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1854, Amherst professor — dead at 29
narrative_blocks.body: Worthington Hooker Jr. graduated from Yale College in 1854 and became Professor of Hygienics and Gymnastics at Amherst College. He died January 26, 1863 at age 29 — one year before the end of the Civil War. His father Worthington Hooker M.D. outlived him by four years, dying in 1867.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: School in New Haven named for his father Worthington Hooker; built 1900, 37 years after his own 1863 death.
School in New Haven named for her father Worthington Hooker; built 1900, 55 years after her own 1845 death.
School in New Haven named for his father Worthington Hooker; built 1900, 61 years after his own 1839 infant death.

narrative_blocks.header: Edward died young
narrative_blocks.body: Edward died in 1839 at the age of one. His parents gave the name to a brother born one year later.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: School in New Haven named for his father Worthington Hooker; built 1900, 59 years after his own 1841 infant death.

narrative_blocks.header: Edward died young
narrative_blocks.body: Edward died in 1841, in his first year.

bio.display_name: Unnamed son of Worthington & Henrietta Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Unnamed died young
narrative_blocks.body: Unnamed died in 1856, in his first year.

narrative_blocks.header: Sole surviving child of Worthington Hooker, 1867
narrative_blocks.body: At his father's death in November 1867, ten-year-old Alfred Edwards Hooker was the only surviving child of six — four siblings by his father's first marriage to Mary Ingersoll had all died before 1867, and his unnamed elder brother by Henrietta had died in infancy. Named for his maternal grandfather Connecticut Governor Henry Waggaman Edwards (HD2045), Alfred lived another twenty years and died in 1887 at age thirty.

notable.notable_blurb: uncle to the medical-textbook author Dr. Worthington Hooker

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, Hampden County MA — returned to family burial ground

education.notes: Yale 1827 — youngest of the four Hooker brothers to attend Yale (John 1810, George 1814, Worthington 1825, Richard 1827).

career.notes: Settled in Macon Georgia after Yale and ordination; ministered there for several decades. His son Thomas was born at Macon in 1849.

narrative_blocks.header: Headed south after Yale, settled in Macon
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale 1827 he was ordained Presbyterian minister and settled in Macon, Bibb County, Georgia — the southernmost outpost of any Hooker brother of his generation. His three older brothers stayed close to Connecticut and Massachusetts in medicine and law; Richard took the gospel south.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Pope of Yale's granddaughter at thirty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: On 15 July 1846 he married Aurelia Dwight at New Haven CT — daughter of James Dwight III and granddaughter of Rev. Timothy Dwight IV, the eighth president of Yale College. The marriage came eighteen years after his Yale graduation and uncle Worthington's later Yale professorship.

narrative_blocks.header: Their son carried two Hooker lines at once
narrative_blocks.body: Their only child Thomas was born at Macon GA in September 1849 — a dual-line Hooker descendant through both parents. From his father Thomas inherited the direct John Hooker descent; from his mother, descent via Mary Hooker's marriage to Rev. James Pierpont in 1690s New Haven, through Sarah Pierpont's marriage to theologian Jonathan Edwards, through Mary Edwards Dwight to Pope of Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at forty-nine in Middletown, buried home
narrative_blocks.body: He died at Middletown, Connecticut on 19 December 1857 at age forty-nine, while north on a visit from Macon. His body was returned to Springfield Cemetery in Hampden County MA to lie with his Hooker parents and Dwight grandparents.

narrative_blocks.header: Posthumously, brother-in-law became Yale's twelfth president
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Aurelia's younger brother Timothy Dwight V was eighteen at the time of Richard's 1846 marriage to her and twenty-nine when Richard died in 1857. Twenty-nine years after Richard's death, Timothy became the twelfth President of Yale in 1886 — the Hooker-Dwight family's second Yale presidency in three generations after Pope Dwight 1795-1817.

parents.mother_name: Susan (Breed) Dwight of New Haven CT

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with parents and Pope of Yale grandfather

narrative_blocks.header: Pope of Yale was her grandfather
narrative_blocks.body: Her father James Dwight III was the third son of Rev. Timothy Dwight IV — the eighth President of Yale College, known to his contemporaries as 'Pope Dwight.' Through her great-grandmother Mary Edwards Dwight (daughter of theologian Jonathan Edwards) she also carried the Yale Edwards line back to Northampton.

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin marriage united two Hooker descents
narrative_blocks.body: Her 1846 marriage to Rev. Richard Hooker brought together two seventh- and eighth-generation descendants of Thomas Hooker through entirely different lines — Richard via direct paternal John Hooker descent, Aurelia via Mary Hooker's 1690s marriage to Rev. James Pierpont and the subsequent Edwards-Dwight succession.

narrative_blocks.header: Survived husband by seventeen years
narrative_blocks.body: Widowed at forty-one in December 1857 when Richard died at Middletown, she returned to her New Haven family home and lived another seventeen years. She died there in January 1874 at fifty-seven and was buried at Grove Street Cemetery near her Dwight grandparents and parents.

cross_connections.display_label: his father-in-law, the famed Springfield publisher

bio.notes: Named "Thomas" — honoring the founding ancestor Thomas Hooker (H00001)

narrative_blocks.header: Carried Thomas Hooker's blood through both parents
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Macon Georgia in September 1849 to Rev. Richard Hooker and Aurelia Dwight, he was the third documented child of a Hooker-Hooker cousin marriage in the tree — and the first whose dual descent propagated biologically. Through his father he descended directly from Thomas Hooker via the John Hooker line of Northampton; through his mother via Mary Hooker Pierpont's 1690s marriage and the Edwards-Dwight succession to Pope of Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Macon-born, married a Springfield Republican daughter
narrative_blocks.body: He was born at Macon Georgia in September 1849, the only child of a Presbyterian minister and a Dwight bride. In 1874 he married Sarah Augusta 'Sally' Bowles of Springfield Massachusetts — daughter of Samuel Bowles III, the editor of the Springfield Republican newspaper, then one of America's most influential dailies.

narrative_blocks.header: Both sons graduated Yale, younger took Harvard Law
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Aurelia died at twenty-four in 1899; his elder son Richard graduated Yale in 1899 the same year of her death; his younger son Thomas Jr graduated Yale in 1903 and went on to Harvard Law in 1906. He himself attended Yale's Sheffield Scientific School for two years without completing the degree.

narrative_blocks.header: Twenty-five years in New Haven banking and trusts
narrative_blocks.body: Returning to New Haven in 1877 after five years of study and travel abroad, he entered business and financial life. He became vice president of the New Haven Trust Company in 1895, its president from 1902 to 1909, and at the merger that created Union & New Haven Trust Company he stepped to vice president there — while simultaneously rising at the First National Bank to vice president 1902, president 1909.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with mother Aurelia, wife Sally, and Pope Dwight forebears

education.notes: Hopkins Grammar School in New Haven for early education — same school where his Dwight great-great-grandfather Pope Dwight had taught after his 1769 Yale graduation.
Yale MA 1872 — three years after BA.
Five years 1872-1877 of study and travel abroad and in this country; returned to New Haven 1877 to enter business and financial career.

career.notes: Vice president of New Haven Trust Company 1895-1902.
Joined First National Bank 1895.
President of New Haven Trust Company 1902-1909, until its merger into Union & New Haven Trust Company.
VP First National Bank 1902-1909.
President of First National Bank from 1909 — held position at time of Carrington 1918 biography.
VP of Union & New Haven Trust Company from 1909 (after merger of NH Trust Co).
Ten years on New Haven Board of Education, 1894-1904.

notable.notable_blurb: Richest man in old Hampshire County

narrative_blocks.header: He ran potash works and an iron foundry
narrative_blocks.body: A Yale graduate and Hatfield-born merchant, Josiah Dwight built his fortune at Westfield and Springfield through trade, a potash manufactory, and an iron foundry. He was reckoned the richest man in the old County of Hampshire at his death in 1768.

narrative_blocks.header: He judged the county court for eighteen years
narrative_blocks.body: From 1750 until his death Dwight sat as a judge of the Court of Common Pleas for Hampshire County and held the rank of lieutenant-colonel in the militia. His estate was inventoried at more than nine thousand pounds.

narrative_blocks.header: A Yale man of the old Dedham Dwights
narrative_blocks.body: Dwight graduated from Yale in 1736 and came of the Dedham Dwights, a first cousin of the grandfather of Yale's president Timothy Dwight. He kept shop at Westfield before establishing himself at Springfield.

narrative_blocks.header: His two daughters married into prominent families
narrative_blocks.body: By his second wife, Elizabeth Buckminster, Dwight had five children who lived to maturity. His daughter Sarah married Judge John Hooker, and his son Josiah carried on the Springfield line.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: He built this Springfield house about 1754; it now stands at Historic Deerfield.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of the Springfield Dwight line
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Buckminster of Brookfield became Josiah Dwight's second wife in 1757 and bore the five children who survived him. She outlived him by thirty years, dying at Springfield in 1798.
